trajectory_summary: This is a short verify-to-DQ call. The rep completed most of the verify section efficiently, identified a disqualifying property type (manufactured home), and exited cleanly. The call's shape is stagnant — no energy was built because the DQ was always the destination once the property type was disclosed. The primary coaching value here is not in the exit itself but in the pre-call screening gap and the missed depth on the customer's primary motivation before the DQ was confirmed.
outcome_dispute: Primary outcome tag is 'unknown' — the correct tag is DQ (disqualified). The manufactured home disclosure at 02:22 triggered a hold and a DQ delivery at 04:34. No appointment was booked or attempted. The call should be tagged as hard_DQ.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: DQ exit language and pre-call screening discipline
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Hey. So it looks like we actually don't work with manufactured homes, so solar may not be the best option for you k.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The manufactured home DQ required an 89-second hold to confirm — a criterion that should be known at the setter level. The exit language ('may not be the best option') is ambiguous rather than definitive, and there is no bridge to any alternative resource. The manager should use this moment to (a) confirm whether manufactured home eligibility is documented in setter training materials and (b) drill the rep on clean DQ exit language that closes the loop for the customer without leaving them in ambiguity. If the criterion isn't documented, escalate to Kenny as a schema gap.

key_moments.quote: There was an ad on the Internet that Colorado gave you 30,000 to put on solar.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the customer's primary stated motivation — and it is factually misconstrued. The customer believes Colorado is giving away $30,000 for solar installs, likely conflating a federal tax credit ad with a cash grant. The rep's response ('Okay. Gotcha. So the the tax incentive?') correctly identifies the category but does not probe the misunderstanding or the emotional driver behind it. When the customer immediately discloses she doesn't file taxes (00:51), the rep says 'You're retired? Okay.' and moves on — leaving the customer's entire motivation anchor unaddressed. Strategic read: the rep may have been deferring the tax credit clarification to the closer. Reactive read: the rep didn't recognize that the customer's primary motivation had just been invalidated and needed a replacement anchor. The reactive read is more supported — there is no subsequent attempt to rebuild motivation around bill savings or any other angle.

This is the customer's second attempt to articulate her interest in solar, and it is nearly inaudible and incomplete — likely 'it seems like a good idea' or similar. The rep's response is 'Cool. Yeah. Yeah. So let me just ask you a few questions.' The schema marks interest_in_solar as the emotional anchor requiring 60+ seconds of depth. The rep spent approximately 3 seconds on both motivation responses combined before pivoting to process. There is no follow-up question, no reflection, no curiosity-building. This is the clearest verify depth gap in the call.

key_moments.quote: The garage is a six car garage, and the house is either manufactured home on a foundation.
key_moments.stewart_read: The customer discloses two property details in a single sentence: a six-car garage and a manufactured home on a foundation. The rep was mid-verify on a direct property question, so both disclosures arrived simultaneously. The manufactured home triggers the DQ path. The six-car garage is a secondary structure that could represent a cross-sell signal (solar on the garage, or roofing on the garage) per the schema's cross_sell_signals section — but the rep's attention correctly shifted to the manufactured home DQ. The cross-sell signal on the garage was never surfaced. This is not a failure of the rep's in-call judgment — the DQ took priority — but it is a missed opportunity that a pre-call screening process would have allowed the rep to think through in advance.

key_moments.quote: Let me check into one thing real quick, Tyler. I'm just gonna put you on hold. I'll be right back. Okay?
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep did not know whether manufactured homes are eligible and needed an 89-second hold to check. This is a knowledge gap, not a scope creep issue — the rep correctly recognized uncertainty and checked rather than guessing. However, the manufactured home DQ criterion should be known at the setter level without a hold. The hold itself is a minor trust cost: the customer waited 89 seconds for a verdict that should have been immediate. The rep also calls the customer 'Tyler' here — the customer's name is Tali (per the opening at 00:03). This is a minor rapport error that goes unaddressed.
